questions.prompt: You need to build a feature that categorizes incoming customer support emails into 5 categories. Volume: 50,000 emails per day. What model should you default to?

choices.text: A reasoning model — categorization needs careful thought.

Start with gpt-5-nano. It's optimized for high-volume simple classification — your cost will be ~$0.001 per email vs $0.05+ per email on full gpt-5. Test quality; move up only if nano measurably underperforms.

questions.explanation: Simple classification is the textbook nano use case. At 50K emails/day, model choice is a 50× cost difference ($1.5K/month on nano vs $75K/month on GPT-5). Always start small for high-volume simple tasks. Reasoning models are overkill — categorization isn't reasoning-heavy.

questions.prompt: When does a reasoning model (gpt-5-thinking, etc.) provide enough benefit to justify the higher cost and latency?

choices.text: When the task is reasoning-heavy: math, complex code correctness, multi-step planning, careful logic. NOT for simple text generation, summarization, formatting, or anywhere latency matters.

questions.explanation: Reasoning models spend 'thinking' tokens before answering — measurably better on hard reasoning problems, but slower and 5–10× more expensive. They're not strictly better; for simple text generation they're slower without quality benefit. Match to task: hard reasoning = reasoning model; simple text = regular model.
